speaker: interviewer
text: Irene, we were talking about the time before you were in the ghetto when you began to understand that things were changing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were describing your father's activities in helping people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yes, my father was helping people from-- that escaped the atrocities from, from Poland and the Ukraines, I imagine, and to help them to get to Palestine. I'm not sure whether they made it to Palestine. But that was the intent. And then after the incidents that I overheard, I never mentioned to anybody in the family or friends about anybody being in our house or talking about awful things that are happening in Poland. And so-- but the pressure was on. And we couldn't do certain things. And, and all this was happening with us. But, of course, nobody believed it because it was told. It wasn't seen yet. And until 1944, it was just-- we were hit so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father allowed to continue with his resort to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In 1943, there was an ordinance issued that all the Jews must give up their businesses. And there were a lot. There were Jews in town that owned-- one-- there was a butcher. They owned the meat market. And then there were a couple of groceries that-- owned by Jews. And my father and his cousins-- my father wasn't the only one in the resort. It was-- most of the families were part of that. And they all worked in there. And they were forced to give it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had the opportunity to choose somebody to give it to before the government took it away from them and just took it away. And hopefully that after the war that they would get back. So they would choose a friend from-- my cousin had a friend in Munkacs. And he owned a hotel in Munkacs. So he traded with him, one of the cousins that was owner of the resort. And they gave the resort to this man. His name was Drutchko, Janosh Drutchko. And he, in turn, gave him the hotel in Munkacs, which was called the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they moved-- my father's cousin moved into Munkacs. And the rest of the family that had bought in their just never acknowledged that it was theirs. They-- everybody had to give it up. So my father was one of the people that, that, that was out of there. And the family that owned-- the, the, the non-Jewish family, the Hungarian family-- they were a big family. They were-- they had three adult children and the mother and father. And they all worked in the resort. So they were capable of taking care of it. And my father said, when this is all over we're going to-- we're going to take it-- we're going to get it back because he's a friend. And he will give it back to us. But that never happened. My father never came back, neither--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect your life at the time? What changes did it--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It, it made me feel like I'm, I'm losing something. I'm not growing anymore. I am at a standstill of something that was very scary. And the fear was, how long is it going to be, never, never thinking that it's going to end in the way that it did. But it's temporary. And it's because there's a war going on. And that's why everybody has to sacrifice. But I didn't understand why the Jews had to give up so much more than anybody else until it was-- I wasn't allowed to question anymore. And then I just-- I just dealt with my feelings. And I did the best I could with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it affect your family materially? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, we weren't tremendously wealthy people. I don't remember any, you know, cars or anything like that. But we had everything. We had-- we had our own home. We had grounds. We had an income. We had food. We had clothing. We were just happy with whatever we had. And when that was starting to be restricted to us-- we couldn't go to the grocery store to get salt or sugar, or-- because everything else pretty much we grew. And we had our own. And then we had to give up some of that stuff, too. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they took, you know, our, our-- my grandfather had a farm. They took that away. He couldn't work that anymore. So there was no hay for the cows. There was no food for the chickens. There was no seeds to plant, you know, vegetables.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very minimized anymore. It was just something that you could do around the house, plant whatever you could in your own backyard and live off that, which was, of course, not enough for two or three families that usually lived off of that. So it, it started to show. And, and also being deprived of your own things that really-- they belonged to you. And they're being taken away. And you worked so hard all your life. You know, my grandfather was very proud of his, his possessions and his being and who he was and where he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's all-- just to watch that generation being so depressed about what was happening. It was-- it was scary because they were our support. They were our tree of, of strength. And, and they were weakening because they were afraid. And it was just very scary for me to see that. But just-- in 1943, my grandmother died. And I, as far back as I can remember, I think it was she died of a stroke. She was the most beautiful little grandmother I can ever picture. She was little and petite. And, and she was just beautiful. And I just loved her. [CRYING] And then, [CRYING] a, a little, short while after that I lost a brother. [CRYING] And he was about five years old. He died of scarlet fever. [CRYING] And-- because again, we couldn't-- we had no access-- [CRYING] we couldn't get to a doctor fast enough to get him medication. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Or we, we just weren't allowed to take care of ourselves. So I lost a brother. And then, then shortly after that, my grandfather died. Again, he had diabetes. And he caught an infection in his toe. And they had to amputate his toe. And, and we couldn't get to a hospital. Or a doctor wouldn't touch him because the Jewish doctors were-- already taken away their practice. Or they weren't allowed to do. And they were, like, too far away. And we couldn't get to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father-- my grandfather died of gangrene because of lack of care. And-- but then, when they took us to-- away, then I was-- I was kind of grateful that he died at home. And he was buried. And he had a funeral. And he has a cemetery. [CRYING] And I can always go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And shortly after that, in-- I believe it was the winter of, of 1944, people were very restless. And they were very afraid. And they were doing a lot of preparations of trying to get news from the world. Because the radios and everything was taken away. We couldn't listen to any radio or read a newspaper. And if anybody came into town from somewhere else, people would kind of hover around them and find out what's going on somewhere else. And everybody was very scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father and my uncle-- my grandparents were gone. And so we had-- and my uncle lived with my grandparents before they died. So he remained in the house. And my father and my uncle, which was his brother, went up in the attic. And they prepared a, a spot in the attic that, that we could go up there and, and spend the days and, and hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we heard that they were taking Jews to other places until after the war. So they, they prepared the place in the attic for us to spend the days there. And the nights, we would be in the house, so nobody would know where we are-- if that-- it comes to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were busy doing that. And they were putting food up there like, they were drying the, the fruit and put dry fruit up there. And they were, like, baking potatoes-- slices on top of the stove and storing it-- and water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day, my father came home. And he said, we cannot leave the house anymore. We will stay in the house. And his friend, which was a policeman in town, he will seal up our house from the outside, so it looks like we were gone. All the people at-- on that day, were taken-- all the Jews-- from town into the schoolhouse. And so nobody was-- and they sealed up everybody's house from the outside. And all the Jews were in the schoolhouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his friend sealed up our house from the outside. And we remained inside. So it looked like we were also gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they took our-- they took-- the, the people from town away to the ghetto the next day. Of course, we didn't know where they went. But they started to bring people from the neighboring towns into the schoolhouse. And from there, they took them either by railroad or by truck. Because the reason my father found out they were bringing everybody to our town is because we had a railroad going to a certain area, which was closer to the ghetto. So that's why they were clearing our town from the Jews first and then brought the other neighboring town-- the Jews from the neighboring towns. And from there, they took them to the ghetto. We-- one, one morning-- and all the-- well, all the Jews, of course, had to wear Jewish stars way before they took them away. And so we had a curfew. But after 6 o'clock nobody was allowed out of the house. And everybody had to go out with the Jewish stars if they went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How early did that begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: That became in '43, in 1943. And I-- my father said, you don't have to wear a Jewish star because you are too young. So we were already sealed in in the house. And all the Jews were in the schoolhouse. And of course, there was no communication. We never had a telephone or anything. So my father said to me, you have to go up to, to the resort and to the bakery. The bakery was not too far from the resort. And you have to find out, you have to bring a newspaper. And you have to find out if the-- if there is any Jewish people in town, and if there is, where they are. And if they are in the schoolhouse, you must ask the baker to take bread to the schoolhouse because they probably are not having any food anymore by now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he put me out in the backyard-- and through the window. And I went through the-- through the-- through the meadows, up-- not through the streets, to the resort. Because it was about a mile or a mile and a half away from the house where that was. And when I got there, my father's friend, which was now the owner of the resort said, what are you doing here? He was yelling at me. They're going to catch you. And they're going to take you away. You can't do this!

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, but my father wants you to give him a newspaper. And he wants to know-- and I, I, I gave him the list that my father gave me to give him. And he says, now listen, I will give you the newspaper. And you hide it. And you go through the railroad. You go through the meadows home. You run-- you run home. And you never come out again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I says, well, where is everybody? He says, in the schoolhouse. I says, my father said to take bread for them because they're hungry. And he says, how do you know they're hungry? And I says, I don't know. My father said they're hungry. You have to take bread. He says, OK, I will try. But  you go home. And you tell your father never to let you out again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went home. And I brought him the newspaper. And he says, no more-- you don't go out. But the next day, see, somebody squealed on us. They told them that we were there. And the next day, they broke the doors open. And we were upstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the gendarme that sealed us in did, did it, because nobody else knew. We didn't think that anybody knew. But-- he did-- he sealed us in. And because he was very mean to us when we were being taken to the-- to the railroad station.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it happen? Do you remember the moment that they came to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We were upstairs. It was during-- it was early in the morning. And we were-- we were already up, up in the attic because daybreak-- we weren't going to have any movements around where they can look into the window and see that somebody is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were upstairs. And we heard the, the doors broke. We had, like, double doors, like, wooden doors besides-- see, it was like glass doors. And then the wooden doors protecting the house from bad weather and storms, and all that. But the wooden doors were, like, sealed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we heard the doors came broken open. And the glass doors-- the glass was shattered. They just-- like, they know exactly where we were. They came right up. We had this big, huge ladder from the pantry of the house up to the attic. And the ladder was moved away. And there was just, like, an attic door to go up. And they knew-- they, they picked up the ladder. And they put it up. They pushed the door up. They came up. And they pushed everybody through the-- like, some of the kids fell down the door. That's how fast they wanted us to get out of there. And they said to my father, in one half hour, you pack all your valuables. And we will be back to get you. And they left one guy there, watching us. But we were already packed. We already had our valuables in certain places. And everybody was told what to take if we have to go. And, and the gendarme-- the-- my father's friend, he was there. And he says, you're going to Tokaj. Tokaj is a place where they make Hungarian wine. And you're going on the vineyard to work on the-- to make wine. So that wasn't so bad. But I think they did that to prevent the panic. And therefore, you have to get your valuables together and take them with you, so you can have means of extra support. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so of course, everybody was packed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was-- I know it was on a third. It was right after Passover because we didn't have time to put away the Passover dishes. So either it was April 3 or May 3, somewheres around that. I really don't remember exact-- the exact date.

speaker: interviewer
text: You celebrated Passover in the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had Passover in the house. But then immediately after that, they, you know, they, they, they started taking all the Jews away. See, everybody was home when we had Passover. But then, like, the day-- the day after Passover, everybody was gone. And then the day after that, they got us. Because we were-- we were prepared. We were up in that attic practicing back and forth before Passover. But then we didn't have time-- it wasn't important anymore to put the Passover dishes away at that moment. And then they-- then, then we didn't have time to put them away because they came and got us. And, and then they took-- they, they broke into the house. They got us out of there. They gave us a half hour. But I don't think that half hour was a half hour. It was less than that. And they marched us out of the house. And then they-- see, we lived on a-- on the street that led-- there was a T. The main streets were a T-- one main street this way, and one this way. And we lived on the T street.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we got to the corner of the other main street, we met with all the other people that were coming from the school. Because the school was-- where they were confined-- was to our right, and-- to our left. And the railroad station was to our right. So we all met everybody on that corner. They were coming down. And they attached us to that. And we went-- all of us went to the railroad station. And then they put us on this little train.

speaker: interviewee
text: But on the route, this was, like-- from the corner into the railroad station, maybe it's a half a mile. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was the longest half a mile in my life because so much bad things were happening on that half a mile. All my friends that I was-- I went to school with, and I was so close with, and I shared things, and I even tried to celebrate Christmas with them, they were standing alongside of the road and yelling, oh, it's about time that you're going. And, and we hate you. And we don't want any Jews here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I could not believe what I was-- what I was seeing. At that moment, I felt that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there is just nothing for me, that wherever I'm going, there-- what is there? My friends hate me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My Jewish friends and family are gone. I don't know where they are. We were with strangers because the people from town left before us. And I felt like I just didn't want to do nothing. I was just disappearing. Little by little, it was just-- I was going away. Pieces of me were just on-- alongside of the road, or something. And then on top of that, I, I-- we were marching in rows and groups. And I walked out of the group. And I was walking alongside. And my father's friend, the gendarme that was leading this group, came-- turned his rifle around, and pushed me into the-- to the group, to the row, and, and called me a dirty name in Hungarian, you know, dirty Jew. And he pushed me into the line and he says, don't ever, ever come out of that line again. And I was going-- I was so stunned because I knew he was my father's friend. They went to school together. And they were so close.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was about to say to him, why are you so mean to me? And my father put his arm-- his hand across my mouth and said to me quietly, from now on you will do as you are told and never answer back anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we, we, we went into-- we got to the railroad station. And we went on an open train. It was like, open wagons. And we went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just a couple of hours, we were on the train. And then we got off the train. And we walked into the ghetto from, from there, through the city. And it was-- Munkacs was a pretty, pretty big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were two ghettos in the city, two brick factories. One was called [HUNGARIAN], which is, in Hungarian, 'a brick factory.' And one was called [HUNGARIAN]. That was the name of the people that owned-- and [HUNGARIAN] is a Jewish name. And probably the other one was, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was abandoned. It was empty. And that was the ghetto. And the buildings that the-- we were in [HUNGARIAN]. And the buildings that, that were there, you know, the, the factory buildings, they were already full with people. So the people there-- when we came, and we had no room, like, to be under a roof. But we ran into my mother's mother. She was already there with my mother's sister-- two sisters and their families. And they had built a little shack, a little hut, from tablecloths and sheets and blankets. And that's where they lived underneath there. And so they had a little room. So we joined them, my mother and my father and my uncle and my siblings. So we lived there. The ghetto was surrounded by water. And on the other side of the water was a, a prison. And it, it looked like a big-- a big mountain. And on top of the mountain, and on each side of the mountain, they had machine guns and guards and-- you know, facing us. Because I guess a lot of people were trying to escape. But the only way they could have done that is go through the water. So-- and because on the other side of the ghetto was the entrance. And, and then-- and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Hungarians who were guarding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Hungarian gendarmes and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] also guards that had, like, brown uniforms. I don't-- I didn't know at that time who they were. But they were-- they were, I think, German, German, like, Gestapo or something. They had brown uniforms, ugly. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and so we were in this ghetto. They-- the next day, they came. And they got all the men and the, the boys away. And they were building the railroads. See, they were building a railroad to come into the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they did that every single, single day. They, they came. They took the men. They had them working all day till late night. They came home. My, my brother and my father were taken. My brother was only 11 years old. And every day, he went with my father, with my uncle. They didn't feed them. They worked on the railroad. They came home, they were like half dead. My mother and my grandfather, all they could do for them is, like, wash them off from their dirt and just make them comfortable. And then the next day, they went back again and, and they-- until-- then the railroad was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had like-- they said that the Red Cross is sending these care packages. So every day, there was a truck that came in with packages. But what really happened is the Jews that lived still in the city made packages and brought it in. And it was distributed for-- and, and, and whatever we had that we brought from home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and that-- when that ran out, it was-- it was no more. People were scavenging. They were in, in, in, I guess, in the trash or in-- or in grass and trying to keep whatever, sharing things, or trying to sell something by the-- by the fence to the outside people, trade it for food. And that's how-- that's all. There was no set place or time for us to go and get food.
